title: Oligomerized protein-polymer conjugates
abstract: The present disclosure relates to protein-polymer conjugates comprising an engineered binding oligomer protein and a polymer for detection of a ligand of interest, methods, compositions and kits thereof.
claims: Claims 1. A protein-polymer conjugate comprising an oligomer of an engineered binding protein linked to a polymer, wherein the protein-polymer conjugate self-assembles into a lamellar nanostructure. 2. The protein-polymer conjugate of claim 1, wherein the polymer comprises a poly(N- isopropylacrylamide) (PNIPAM) block, a poly(hydroxypropyl acrylate) (PHPA) block, a poly(oligoethylene glycol acrylate) (POEGA) block, or a poly(3-(2-methacroyloyethyl)-N,N- dimethylammonio)propane sulfonate) (PDMAS) block. 3. The protein-polymer conjugate of claims 1 or 2, wherein the protein oligomers comprise trimers and/or tetramers of the binding protein. 4. The protein-polymer conjugate of any one of claims 1-3, wherein the binding protein is a Sso7d protein, a Sac7d protein, an engineered coiled-coil protein, or an antibody variable domain. 5. The protein-polymer conjugate of claim 4, wherein the Sso7d protein is a recombinant (rc) rcSso7d protein engineered to bind a ligand. 6. The protein-polymer conjugate of claim 5, wherein the rcSso7d protein comprises at least 85% of the amino acid sequence of SEQ ID NO: 2 from Sulfolobus solfataricus. 7. The protein-polymer conjugate of claim 5, wherein the ligand is streptavidin or a monomeric variant of streptavidin. 8. The protein-polymer conjugate of claim 4, wherein the coiled-coil protein is a three- helix bundle protein. 9. The protein-polymer conjugate of claim 4, wherein the antibody variable domain is a VH, VHH, and/or VL domain. 10. The protein-polymer conjugate of claim 4, wherein the oligomer of the antibody variable domain is an scFv. 11. The protein-polymer conjugate of any one of claims 1-9, wherein the binding proteins are linked by peptide linkers. 12. The protein-polymer conjugate of claim 11, wherein the peptide linkers comprise Gly- Ser linkers. 13. A biosensor comprising the protein-polymer conjugate of any one of claims 1-12 bound to a surface. 14. The biosensor of claim 13, wherein the protein-polymer conjugate forms a thin film on the surface. 15. The biosensor of claim 14, wherein the thin film comprises at least 2-fold more binding sites in an area of the protein-polymer conjugate compared to a monolayer of the protein of the same size as the area of the protein-polymer conjugate. 16. The biosensor of any one of claims 13-15, wherein the limit of detection for binding by the protein is between 100 nM and 300 nM. 17. The biosensor of any one of claims 13-16, wherein the surface is a semiconductor material, a quartz material, a glass material, a paper material, a cellulose material, or a nitrocellulose material. 18. The biosensor of claim 17, wherein the surface is silicon. 19. The biosensor of any one of claims 13-18, wherein the unit spacing between repeating domains of protein-polymer conjugates on the surface is less than 50 nm. 20. The biosensor of claim 19, wherein the unit spacing between repeating domains of protein-polymer conjugates is less than 30 nm. 21. A method for making a protein-polymer conjugate comprising conjugating a protein to a polymer and dissolving the protein-polymer conjugate in aqueous solution. 22. The method of claim 21, wherein the polymer is in molar excess of the protein. 23. The method of claim 22, wherein the polymer is in at least 5-fold molar excess of the protein. 24. The method of any one of claims 21-23, wherein the conjugating is in the presence of a cross-linking agent. 25. The method of claim 24, wherein the cross-linking agent is: (i) an aldehyde, optionally glutaraldehyde or formaldehyde; or (ii) ultraviolet light and Ru(BiPy) 3 +2 . 26. A method for detecting a ligand of interest, the method comprising: (a) contacting a biosensor of any one of claims 13-20 with a sample comprising a ligand of interest, wherein the engineered binding protein oligomer binds the ligand of interest; and (b) detecting the ligand of interest bound by the engineered binding protein oligomer. 27. The method of claim 26, wherein the protein-polymer conjugate is in molar excess of the ligand of interest. 28. The method of claims 26 or 27, wherein the protein-polymer conjugate is in at least lO-fold molar excess of the ligand of interest. 29. The method of any one of claims 26-28, wherein the ligand of interest is streptavidin or monomeric streptavidin. 30. The method of any one of claims 26-29, wherein the sample is a biological sample from a subject. 31. The method of claim 30, wherein the sample is blood, serum, or urine. 32. The method of claim 30, wherein the subject is a mammal. 33. The method of claim 32, wherein the subject is a human. 34. The method of any one of claims 26-33, wherein the unit spacing between repeating domains of engineered binding protein oligomers on the surface is less than 50 nm. 35. The method of claim 34, wherein the unit spacing between repeating domains of engineered binding protein oligomers is less than 30 nm. 36. The method of any one of claims 26-35, wherein the molecular weight of the ligand of interest is less than 50 kDa. 37. The method of claim 36, wherein the molecular weight of the ligand of interest is less than 25 kDa. 38. A method for detecting a ligand of interest, the method comprising: (a) contacting the protein-polymer conjugate of any one of claims 1-12 with a sample comprising a ligand of interest, wherein the ligand of interest binds to the engineered binding protein oligomer and forms a complex; (b) contacting the complex with a surface for a time sufficient for the complex to bind the surface; and (c) detecting the ligand of interest bound by the engineered binding protein oligomer. 39. The method of claim 38, wherein the protein-polymer conjugate is in solution. 40. The method of claims 38 or 39, wherein the solution comprises a buffer. 41. The method of any one of claims 38-40, wherein the sample is a biological sample. 42. The method of claim 41, wherein the biological sample is blood, serum or urine. 43. The method of any one of claims 38-42, wherein the protein-polymer conjugate is in molar excess of the ligand of interest. 44. The method of claim 43, wherein the protein-polymer conjugate is in at least lO-fold molar excess of the ligand of interest. 45. The method of any one of claims 38-44, wherein the ligand of interest is streptavidin or monomeric streptavidin. 46. The method of any one of claims 38-45, wherein the surface is a semiconductor material, a quartz material, a glass material, a paper material, a cellulose material, or a nitrocellulose material. 47. The method of any one of claims 38-46, wherein the surface is silicon. 48. The method of any one of claims 38-47, wherein the sample is a biological sample from a subject. 49. The method of claim 48, wherein the subject has or is suspected of having an infectious disease. 50. The method of claims 48 or 49, wherein the subject is a mammal. 51. The method of claim 50, wherein the subject is a human. 52. The method of any one of claims 38-51, wherein the engineered binding protein oligomer binds to streptavidin or monomeric streptavidin. 53. The method of any one of claims 38-52, wherein the limit of detection for binding by the protein is between 100 nM and 300 nM. 54. The method of any one of claims 38-53, wherein unit spacing between repeating domains of engineered binding protein oligomers in the complex is less than 50 nm. 55. The method of claim 54, wherein the unit spacing between repeating domains of engineered binding protein oligomers is less than 30 nm. 56. The method of any one of claims 38-55, wherein the molecular weight of the ligand of interest is less than 50 kDa. 57. The method of claim 56, wherein the molecular weight of the ligand of interest is less than 25 kDa. 58. A kit for assessing a presence or amount of a ligand, the kit comprising a container containing the protein-polymer conjugate of any one of claims 1-12. 59. The kit of claim 58, further comprising a surface for binding the protein-polymer conjugate. 60. The kit of claim 58 or 59, wherein the protein-polymer conjugate is bound to the surface. 61. The kit of claim 58 or 59, wherein the protein-polymer conjugate is not bound to the surface. 62. The kit of any one of claims 58-61, wherein the surface is a semiconductor material, a quartz material, a glass material, a paper material, a cellulose material, or a nitrocellulose material. 63. The kit of claim 62, wherein the surface is silicon. 64. A method of detecting a ligand of interest, the method comprising: (a) contacting the biosensor any one of claims 13-20 with a sample comprising a ligand of interest, wherein the ligand of interest binds to the protein-polymer conjugate and forms a complex; and (b) detecting the ligand of interest bound by the protein-polymer conjugate. 65. The method of claim 64 wherein the sample is a biological sample. 66. The method of claim 65, wherein the biological sample is blood, serum, or urine. 67. The method of claims 65 or 66, wherein the biological sample is from a subject having or suspected of having an infectious disease. 68. The method of claim 67, wherein the subject is mammal. 69. The method of claim 68, wherein the subject is a human. 70. The method of any one of claims 64-69, wherein the protein-polymer conjugate is in molar excess of the ligand of interest. 71. The method of claim 70, wherein the protein-polymer conjugate is in at least lO-fold molar excess of the ligand of interest. 72. The method of any one of claims 64-71, wherein the ligand of interest is streptavidin or monomeric streptavidin. 73. The method of any one of claims 64-72, wherein the limit of detection is 5 nM - 300 nM.
